contributions.content: Has anyone reported this to highways? It has been at least three years since the last attempt.
I reported it twice last year. Got an automated acknowledgement both times, nothing since.
The drain is sized for 1970s rainfall volumes. This is not a maintenance issue — it needs redesigning.
This has hit elderly residents hardest. Many cannot afford taxis and have simply stopped going out.
The council cited low passenger numbers. Those numbers were already low because the timetable had been unreliable for two years before cancellation.
Good to see this written up as a concrete proposal. £40k for a drain upgrade is consistent with similar projects I have seen.
Worth flagging that active travel grants could cover the cycle and lighting element. The combined scope might cost less net than the drain alone.
What is the process for commissioning an engineering assessment? Does it go through the council directly?
Yes, highways assessments go through the council. A request from a resident group carries more weight than individual submissions.
The lot has been sitting empty for four years. This is exactly the kind of use it should be put to.
Who holds the freehold? If it is the council the process is straightforward. If it is a private owner it is more complex.
I would volunteer for the resident committee if this moves forward.
I walked past at 7:45 this morning. Three of the seven lamps were completely out and two more were flickering. This is genuinely dangerous.
The council's response time for street lighting faults is supposed to be five working days. It has been over four months. That needs to be escalated.
There is a formal complaint route to the highways department. If enough residents submit individually it creates a paper trail that is harder to ignore.
Before we go that route, does anyone know if there is a ward councillor surgery coming up? Direct pressure at that level is often faster.
The next surgery is in three weeks. I am happy to attend if others will come too. A group showing up is more visible than one person.
The northern section near the gate is the worst. There is a root heave that has lifted a whole slab and left a drop of about four centimetres. That is a serious trip hazard even for ambulant users.

My mother in law uses the park every day. She has had to stop going on her own because she does not feel safe on the path. That is an unacceptable loss of independence caused by basic maintenance failure.
The Equality Act is relevant here. Publicly accessible outdoor space managed by or on behalf of a public body has to be reasonably accessible. This falls short of that standard.
Sport England has a specific small grants programme for inclusive access improvements. I can share the details if someone wants to lead a bid.
120 young people is not a small number. That is a substantial chunk of the secondary school age population in this area. The decision was made with no public process at all.
I asked the council for the budget decision paper. They cited a general savings target — there was no specific assessment of the impact on young people. That should have happened.
The building is already paid for. The question is just who runs the programme. There must be volunteers here who could hold something together in the short term.
I spoke to two of the young people who used it regularly. They were not angry, they were just defeated. That is worse in a way. They expected this.
A meanwhile licence from the council costs almost nothing to negotiate. If the building is sitting empty anyway they have little reason to refuse.
Thirteen empty units is beyond normal churn. At this point the vacancy itself is the problem — prospective occupiers see the boards and choose somewhere else.
The BID has been saying it will address this for two years. Nothing has changed. At this point residents need to push directly.

Some of these landlords are holding out for retail rents that simply are not coming back. Meanwhile use at a peppercorn is better for them than an empty unit attracting squatters and business rates liability.
There is a High Street vacancy levy in the consultation stage nationally. That may change landlord incentives within a couple of years. Meanwhile use buys time.

We have been running the sensor network for eight months now. The pattern is consistent — exceedances cluster on weekday mornings when the logistics operators are running most of their vehicles. This is not random background variation.
I have the full dataset. Happy to share it in a format that can go in a formal Environmental Health submission if someone wants to lead that.
The school is the most concerning element. NO2 at those concentrations during the school run is directly harmful to developing lungs. That has to be the framing for any public pressure.
When the operators expanded two years ago there was supposed to be a transport management plan. I submitted a freedom of information request and it does not appear to exist.
That is significant. A planning condition with no compliance document is an enforcement matter. The council's planning enforcement team can be asked to investigate.
I time the gaps every morning. At peak times the longest safe gap is about twenty seconds and they come maybe once every three minutes. Most people just take a risk.
The school keeps an incident log. I asked the office and they confirmed two near-misses in the last term were recorded. That formal evidence should be attached to any application.
I drive this road sometimes. Even as a driver I can see the problem. Sight lines at the bend are poor and pedestrians are almost invisible until you are very close.

Pedestrian crossing applications go through Highways. But they carry out their own assessment and can deprioritise requests that do not meet their threshold. The school incident log is important evidence.
If the formal application is slow, a temporary courtesy crossing with volunteers during pick-up and drop-off is a practical option in the meantime. Other schools have done this.

The warm space function is not acknowledged anywhere in the council's decision. I sat in the library on a cold Tuesday in January. There were eleven people there who had nowhere else to go. That was invisible in the budget paper.

The computer terminals are also not replaceable for people without home internet. Job applications, universal credit claims, GP registrations — these require online access. Cutting the hours cuts access to all of that.
I made a formal information request about the consultation process. The council confirmed there was none. That may be a legal issue as well as a democratic one.
The Carnegie UK Trust has produced extensive evidence on the community value of libraries. That evidence base is exactly what a community trust application would be built on.
Is anyone already in contact with libraries that have successfully transitioned to a community trust model? Learning from their experience would save a lot of time.
The Frome Community Library in Somerset did this successfully. They went from closure threat to a thriving community trust within eighteen months. Worth contacting them.
LED replacement is the right call regardless of the maintenance backlog. The running cost reduction over ten years will more than offset the capital outlay.
The school gate extension is crucial. The current zone ends fifty metres short of where children are actually walking. That gap needs to be explicitly in the brief.
Has anyone checked whether the columns themselves need replacing or just the fittings? If the columns are sound, retrofitting LED heads is significantly cheaper.
Good point. A condition survey before specifying the works would clarify that. Should be part of the initial assessment.
The audit-first approach is right. Without knowing the full picture, money could be spent on visible sections while the worst-rated inaccessible sections are left untouched.
The Sport England application needs a community champion with experience of the process. Does anyone here have grant writing background?
I have written three successful Heritage Lottery Fund applications. The process is similar. Happy to help draft the Sport England bid if the group wants to move forward.
The phased approach makes this achievable within a single budget cycle. Getting the northern loop done first would restore access for the majority of users relatively quickly.
A meanwhile licence is genuinely quick to set up if the council is cooperative. The key is getting a named officer who will champion it internally rather than it sitting in a queue.
The young people themselves should be involved in designing the programme from the start, not just consulted on a draft that adults have already written.
The local football club runs holiday sessions. They might be willing to extend to weekday evenings if there is a space available.
Worth contacting the secondary school too. They may be able to provide DBS-checked volunteers and could see this as supporting their pastoral remit.
A steering group meeting in the next two weeks would be worth setting up before summer. Does anyone want to host?
The BID needs to be a genuine partner here, not just a name on the letterhead. They have direct relationships with the landlords that make this possible.
Landlords are often more open to meanwhile use than people assume. The business rates liability on an empty unit is real. A meanwhile tenant takes that off their hands.
Five units in six months is achievable if the meanwhile licence template is already drafted and the council has pre-authorised the scheme. That groundwork matters.
A repair café, a community freezer, and a youth drop-in would each serve completely different populations and together would make the High Street a place people actually go.
Twenty sensors is the right scale for full coverage. Six was enough to establish the pattern. Twenty produces data that cannot be argued away.
The open dashboard matters beyond the legal case. It gives residents ongoing visibility and creates public pressure that outlasts any single complaint submission.
The 28-day response request gives the council a clear deadline. If they miss it that is itself relevant to any subsequent legal action.
We should be careful to get the methodology documented properly so it can withstand challenge. Low-cost sensors have known calibration limitations. That needs to be addressed head on.
Agreed. Co-locating reference monitors alongside the community sensors at three points would anchor the calibration. Some universities will do this as a research partnership.
The signalised crossing is the correct solution. A zebra crossing on a road with that level of traffic and that sight-line problem is not sufficient.
The near-miss documentation from the school is crucial. Highways assessments are heavily evidence-weighted. Two documented incidents in one term is significant.
I can write up a formal letter to the highways team on behalf of a resident group if people are willing to co-sign. A group submission is weighted more heavily.
Worth copying the ward councillor and the local MP. A school road crossing is exactly the kind of visible local issue that elected representatives can move quickly on.
The School Streets pilot is worth pursuing in parallel. Even if it fails at the Traffic Regulation Order stage, the pilot data would support the crossing application.
The community trust model is well-established now. There are templates for the governance structure and the lease agreement. We do not need to start from scratch.
The legal challenge and the trust model are not in tension if they are handled by separate working groups. One group negotiates the lease, another pursues the consultation question.

The warm space, digital access, and social prescribing framing is genuinely important. It shifts the library from a discretionary service to an essential community infrastructure argument. That is much harder for the council to dismiss.

NHS social prescribing teams are actively looking for community spaces to refer to. A conversation with the Primary Care Network before the trust is formally established would open that funding door early.
Can we get a meeting with the head of the Carnegie UK Trust's library programme? They have supported transitions like this before and could be a lead funder as well as an adviser.

The online booking system only works if you have a smartphone and know how to use it. My neighbour is 78 and has been turned away twice. The digital-only approach is a deliberate exclusion of older patients.
The three-month wait for a routine appointment means people are showing up at A&E with conditions that could have been caught earlier. This will cost the NHS more, not less.
This is a contracted service failure. The integrated care board sets the access standards and the practices are contracted to meet them. There is a formal complaint route.
I know two people who have deferred treatment because they could not get an appointment. One is now dealing with a significantly worse situation as a result.
The station argument that it is not their responsibility and the council argument that it is not theirs leaves these people in a gap between institutions that simply does not serve them.
Outreach services can achieve a great deal if they are properly resourced and are allowed to work at the pace of the person, not the institution. Neither of those conditions is currently met.
The referral system exists. It just is not being acted on. That is not a capacity problem — it is a prioritisation problem. Someone has decided this is not urgent.

The car park was not just convenient. For the accessible bays it was the only option that worked for many blue badge holders. The gradient on the Riverside car park excludes anyone with significant mobility difficulties.
The council received £3.2 million for that site. A fraction of that could fund a replacement short-stay facility. Has anyone asked where the money is going?
Businesses on the high street are already reporting a drop in custom from customers who used to use Bridge Street. This will cost the local economy more than the sale generated.
The seniors' lunch club has been running for eleven years. It has never had to close before. Eleven years of community infrastructure closed by a boiler failure that should have been fixed in a week.
The council has a duty of care to the community groups using this building. Allowing it to remain unusable for three months is not acceptable.
Has anyone contacted the council's voluntary sector support team? They sometimes have emergency discretionary funds for exactly this kind of situation.
The mental health peer support group moving to a commercial venue at £80 per session is simply not sustainable. They will run out of money in a few weeks and the group will collapse.
Victorian industrial buildings are almost never structurally unsound. They were overengineered by modern standards. The assumption that it needs to be demolished has not been tested.
The planning system has specific protections for unlisted buildings of merit in conservation areas. Someone needs to check whether the Old Mill is within or adjacent to any designated area.
There are several successful conversions of similar mill buildings elsewhere. Workspace, housing, cultural venues. The developer is choosing the lowest-effort option, not the only one.
I have contacted the local history society. They are keen to be involved and may have documentation supporting a heritage significance statement.
The food bank was designed for emergencies. When emergencies become the permanent condition for hundreds of households, the model itself has to change. We cannot keep patching.
Referral agencies are using the food bank as a first resort because their own provision has collapsed. We are absorbing demand that should be met by statutory services.
The community pantry model is worth researching seriously. It is operating successfully in several UK towns and the dignity argument is compelling — members are customers, not recipients.
The 300 percent increase in demand is not a food bank problem. It is evidence of a broader failure. The food bank is where that failure becomes visible.
The Ofsted report identified this five years ago. Five years of children standing inside at break time because of an inadequate surface. This is not a new problem — it is a neglected one.
The physical activity implications are significant. Children who cannot play outside at school are less active, which affects concentration, sleep, and long-term health.
Section 106 money for play and physical activity in this ward has not been fully allocated. Someone needs to check the position with the council planning team.

Eleven months in and we have had one telephone consultation. No plan, no timeline, no interim support. The GP told us at the referral that we would wait. We did not understand what that actually meant.
School pastoral staff are carrying young people in crisis because there is nowhere else. They are doing it out of care, but they are not trained for it and they should not have to.
The ICB publishes waiting time data quarterly. The last report showed CAMHS at 18.2 months average wait. That is almost double the NHS target. Where is the scrutiny?
We found a private therapist but at £65 a session it is not sustainable. This is a service that should be free. We are paying because the state has stepped back.
I have been on the waiting list for four years and seven months. I have moved twice and changed jobs since I applied. The allotment I applied for has become something I dream about.
The Greenfield Road site is council-owned, has been unused for years, and has road access. It is a straightforward case for development. The question is whether there is political will.

Allotments are not a luxury. The evidence on food growing for physical and mental health is strong. The council's own public health strategy references it. The waiting list is a contradiction of the council's own stated priorities.
I have started wearing earplugs to bed. That should not be a requirement in my own home. The noise on Friday and Saturday nights is relentless until past 3 am.
The council's monitoring visits have both been on weekday evenings. The problem is at weekends after midnight. The monitoring is either incompetent or deliberate.
The licence allows amplified music until midnight. The noise continues until 3 am. That is a breach. The council has the power to revoke the licence. Why has it not acted?
I have kept a log of every night with a noise incident since November. Timestamps and descriptions. If anyone wants to combine logs before a formal submission, contact me.

I have a medical appointment I have been unable to attend for six weeks because of the lifts. The housing association's response has been to send engineers who fix it temporarily and then it breaks again. That is not a repair — it is a cycle.

The association's legal obligation is to carry out repairs within a reasonable time. Three months is not reasonable for a safety-critical item. There is a compensation route through the Housing Ombudsman.
Four wheelchair users are housebound. This is not an inconvenience — it is a deprivation of liberty. The framing matters for any legal or regulatory action.
I have written three formal letters and received three acknowledgements. No repair, no timeline, no alternative offered. The paper trail is there if we need it.
The disability cricket team has been training in a car park for two months. The adaptations they have made to play on an accessible surface are exactly what the pitch should have been providing.
Six clubs, six sets of members, six sets of families affected. Between them they represent a significant number of residents. A collective approach to the council would carry real weight.
The pitch generates income for the parks budget. If the parks department understood that every week of closure is a direct revenue loss, it might move faster.
Planning enforcement has the power to require removal of fly-posting on listed buildings. It has had that power for six months and has not used it once. There is no shortage of evidence.
The stone damage from chemical cleaning is irreversible. Once that texture and surface is gone it cannot be restored. The cost of non-enforcement is being paid in heritage loss.
A listing certificate changes what enforcement powers apply and how seriously the council is required to treat damage. That is the most durable protection.
I have started photographing every instance as it appears and dating the photo. If we pool those records we have a documented pattern that enforcement cannot dismiss as anecdotal.
There is a formal process for designating banking deserts under the Financial Conduct Authority's access to cash rules. This area may qualify. Has anyone looked at the criteria?

The two payday lenders that have opened charge APRs of over 1000 percent. For residents who cannot access mainstream credit, those are the only options. The banks have created the market they are now absent from.
The Banking Hub initiative specifically targets areas that have lost their last branch. We may qualify. The application goes through Link and requires evidence of community need.

The loss of the FM licence is permanent. Once Westgate FM goes off-air the frequency returns to Ofcom and any future community radio in this area starts from zero. That is eleven years of trust and audience gone.
Arts Council made this decision as part of a portfolio rebalancing, not because the station failed. That context matters for any alternative funding application — this is not a failed project.

The young people who have gone through the media training programme are now working in radio, television, and journalism. That is a ten-year track record of community investment that should count for something with funders.
I am in contact with the station director. They are exploring every option but they need community support and resources they do not have. Anyone who can contribute time or skills, please reach out.

I have walked Boundary Field every week for years. It is not just open space — it has significant wildflower grassland and an established hedgerow that provides a wildlife corridor. Losing it is an ecological loss as well as a community one.

The legal basis for the green belt disposal is the question. The cabinet paper cites financial pressures as exceptional circumstances. That has been challenged successfully elsewhere through judicial review.
The planning application is live. The five-week representation window is the opportunity to put concerns formally on record. We need to mobilise as many residents as possible to submit.
The 60 percent increase was introduced with no consultation with traders. The council simply announced the new rates. After eighteen years on this market that is a serious breach of trust.
Three of the five traders who have left had been here for over twenty years. That is not churn — that is the destruction of something that took generations to build.
A market with twelve stalls instead of seventeen looks half empty. Perception drives reality in retail. The council's fee increase is making the viability problem worse, not better.
The remaining traders need to act collectively. Individually we are easy to ignore. Together we can negotiate, and if needed, demonstrate publicly.

I tried to submit a comment on a planning application last month. It took me forty minutes to create an account and I still could not find the right form. That is not accessible — it is deliberately obscure.

The residents I support are not technophobic — they simply have not grown up with these systems. An hour of help would enable them to participate. They are currently excluded by complexity, not incapacity.
The council's public sector equality duty requires accessible services. If the planning portal systematically excludes residents by age or digital literacy, that is a potential Equality Act issue.
The patch the council applied lasted six weeks before it broke up again. That is not maintenance — it is a temporary cosmetic fix that delays the proper repair while increasing the total cost.

Three incidents in seven months on the same stretch of road. If a council knew about a pavement defect that caused three slips and injuries, they would face serious liability questions. The same logic applies here.
The underlying road surface is failing. No amount of patching will fix that. The council needs to understand that the longer they defer the proper repair, the more expensive it becomes.
My mother budgets her heating around this payment. She received the letter saying it would not come and had to immediately work out how to cover the gap from a pension that has no spare capacity.
The equality impact assessment was not completed before this decision was taken. That is procedurally wrong. The council has to understand who is affected before it cuts a benefit like this.

There are seven months before the next payment would have been due. That is not a long window but it is enough time to seek replacement funding from charitable sources or to negotiate a partial reinstatement.
The council's own data shows 400 eligible households. It knows who these people are. It could write to them with information about alternative support. That would cost almost nothing.
The hub model works where it has been properly resourced. The risk is that it becomes a way to rationalise further reductions in individual practice capacity rather than genuinely adding access.

A pharmacist in the hub for medication queries and minor ailment assessment would significantly reduce GP demand. Many appointments that currently require a GP can be handled by a clinical pharmacist.
The ICB has an access improvement fund. The challenge is that the application has to be led by a GP practice or primary care network, not a community group. We need a clinical partner.

The specialist homelessness charities operating in this area have existing relationships with the individuals at the station. Starting from scratch with a new provider wastes those relationships. Whoever we commission needs to engage the existing organisations.
Seven days a week is the right specification. Rough sleeping does not take weekends off. Provision that is only available on weekdays leaves the most dangerous nights uncovered.
The referral coordinator idea in the amendment is important. The barrier is rarely outreach — it is what happens after the outreach worker hands over to housing. That is where people get lost.

The Victoria Street service yard has been unused for three years. The council will need to carry out basic safety and drainage checks before it can be used for public parking. That could take a few weeks.
A meanwhile licence can be structured to run for as long as the permanent solution takes. If we get this right it buys the council the time it needs to plan a proper replacement.
The accessible bays must be included from day one. Retrofitting them later is expensive and the community that most needs this facility cannot afford to wait for phase two.

The emergency fund idea is sound but it needs a clear governance structure. Who decides which buildings qualify? The council cannot simply direct money to whichever building generates the most complaints.
The energy efficiency requirement is the right condition. Buildings that receive emergency repair grants should not be in the same position again in five years.
I looked into this. If the council holds the freehold and the lease includes a repairing obligation on the council, the boiler repair may not be a grant at all — it may be a legal obligation.
A community energy scheme feasibility study alongside the repair fund makes sense. The running cost reduction would make these buildings more viable in the long term.
The listing application needs to be submitted before the demolition date. That is a hard deadline. Every week spent deliberating is a week closer to the point where intervention is impossible.
Local listing through the council is faster than Historic England designation. It does not prevent demolition but it triggers a more formal assessment before any planning consent would be granted.
The structural survey is the key. If we can demonstrate that the building is sound, the demolition argument collapses. Victorian industrial buildings are almost always reusable.

The community pantry model requires a fundamental shift in how the operation is managed — from a charity giving to recipients to a service that members use. The volunteer team needs to be part of designing that transition.
The council partnership on premises is the critical dependency. Without a suitable building at low cost, the pantry model does not work financially.
Supermarket partnerships for stock donation are essential. The pantry model only works if the value in the weekly shop significantly exceeds the member contribution.

The cooking skills element in the amendment makes the pantry into a genuine community resource rather than just a dignified food bank. That distinction matters for how funders and council partners see it.
The parent fundraising group needs to be established quickly. The next academic year is not far away and the planning, procurement, and installation takes time once the money is in place.
The Schools Playing Fields Association grant is worth pursuing. They have funded similar projects and the application is relatively straightforward.
The Section 106 route needs a named officer to pursue it. That is not something parents can do alone — we need the school to formally request the allocation on our behalf.

The peer support model works when it is genuinely peer-led — by young people who have been through similar experiences, not by adults acting as proxies. That distinction matters enormously for trust and attendance.
The school pastoral team are already in relationships with these young people. Any programme that bypasses those relationships will struggle to recruit participants.
The ICB accountability amendment is as important as the peer programme itself. If we build provision that absorbs the demand, the pressure on CAMHS is reduced and the waiting times never improve.
The site assessment is the first step. Until we have a formal assessment it is all speculation. Someone needs to formally request it from the council's parks and open spaces team.
The raised bed requirement in the amendment should be in the design brief from the start. Retrofitting accessible infrastructure onto a completed site always costs more and works less well.

The therapeutic growing programme idea is genuinely innovative. If the horticultural therapy charity is willing to partner, this could attract health funding that goes well beyond what allotments alone would qualify for.
The acoustic assessment needs to be commissioned independently, not by the venue. Any assessment the venue commissions itself will not be trusted by residents or the licensing authority.

The liaison group amendment is the most sustainable long-term solution. If residents and operators can work together, most of these issues can be resolved without the licensing authority needing to intervene.
The licensing review requires evidence. The complaint log, combined with an independent acoustic assessment taken at the right times and locations, should be sufficient to request a formal review.

The Housing Ombudsman route is the right immediate step. They can direct the association to carry out repairs and award compensation to affected residents. The process is accessible to residents directly.

The Regulator of Social Housing referral is the right longer-term step. If this association is failing on lift maintenance it is probably failing on other things too. A regulatory inspection would reveal that.

The residents most affected — those who are housebound — need advocacy support. Not everyone has the capacity to pursue complaints and legal routes when they are also dealing with the health consequences of isolation.

The cooperative model gives the clubs a collective voice they currently lack. Individually we are tenants of a council pitch with no governance rights. Together we are a body that can hold a lease and invest.
The Sport England application is worth pursuing seriously. The cooperative governance structure is exactly what they prefer and the facilities improvement case is strong.
Floodlighting is the transformative element. Evening use would double the income-generating hours and allow the junior leagues to run fixtures that currently have to be cancelled when daylight ends.
The volunteer steward programme is a good first step but it addresses the consequence, not the cause. The listing application is the durable intervention.
Historic England can move quickly on listing applications where demolition risk is documented and the building has clear heritage significance. We need to compile that evidence now.

The conservation area designation is also relevant here. Any works to unlisted buildings within a conservation area require conservation area consent, which the council controls. That is a lever that currently is not being used.

The Banking Hub application goes through Link, the cash access coordination body. It is a formal process and requires evidence of community need — which we have. Someone needs to take on the coordination role.

The credit union element is as important as the banking hub. For residents who cannot access mainstream credit, the credit union provides ethical lending that prevents them being forced to payday lenders.
The debt advice colocation is the right addition. The residents who need a banking hub also disproportionately need debt advice. One visit can address both needs.
The six-week deadline is real. The fundraising campaign needs to start this week, not next week. Every day is a day closer to the point where keeping the transmitter running is no longer possible.
The Community Radio Fund has a fast-track emergency grant process. The station needs to apply immediately — not wait for the governance review to complete first.
The CIC transition is the right long-term direction. Community shares would allow listeners to invest and create a funding model that is not dependent on a single grant relationship.
A community land trust is the right vehicle but it takes time to establish. The planning representation window is five weeks. We need to do both simultaneously, not sequentially.

The exceptional circumstances argument is the legal foundation for challenging the sale. Cabinet papers citing 'financial pressures' as exceptional circumstances have been successfully challenged before. We need a solicitor's opinion.
The ecological survey is important but it needs to happen within the representation window. Does anyone have contacts with ecology surveyors who could move quickly?

The cooperative needs a legal structure from day one. An informal collective of traders cannot hold a lease or open a bank account. We need to register as a cooperative society or CIC before we can proceed.
The reserved pitch for community stalls is the right signal. It changes the market from a purely commercial space to a community resource. That is a different conversation with the council.
The first task is to get the remaining traders to agree to cooperate. That requires a meeting. I am happy to organise it if people are willing to come.
The planning portal is the most urgent use case because the representation window is time-limited. If residents cannot submit planning comments, they lose that right entirely for that application.
The college partnership is the most sustainable model. If we rely on ad hoc volunteers the service will be inconsistent and will probably collapse within a year.
The device lending scheme is the piece that makes everything else work. A one-hour drop-in session is not enough if residents have no device to use between visits.
Connecting this to the library trust proposal makes strategic sense. A digital inclusion service strengthens the trust's funding case and the warm space argument simultaneously.

The bioswale design looks good to me in principle, but I'm concerned about the maintenance commitment. Who is responsible for keeping the planting alive after installation? If no one claims that, we'll have a dead bioswale within two years and the council won't touch it.

Agreed — this needs to be in the proposal explicitly. My suggestion: the Drainage Action Group takes on a three-year maintenance commitment as a condition of the consent-to-excavate. We document it, it goes in the project record, and we treat it as a standing resource requirement.

I can commit to leading on maintenance for the first season if we get the planting right. Native species are much more forgiving than ornamentals. The key is not installing anything that needs irrigation once established.
Has anyone consulted with the Riverside Quarter on this? The Elm Street underpass drains toward the river and there may be Environment Agency considerations we haven't accounted for.
Good point. I'll reach out to the EA officer who covers this stretch. We should have that conversation before finalising the design regardless.

Before we organise the clearance day, we need to establish what category of waste is on the site. If there's anything classified as hazardous — asbestos sheeting, tyres, chemical containers — we can't handle it ourselves. Has anyone done a visual assessment recently?

I walked the site last week. There are bagged household waste, construction rubble, and a couple of mattresses. I didn't see anything that looked obviously hazardous but I'm not qualified to say for certain. We should get someone with COSHH awareness to do a proper look before the day.

I can do that — I have my COSHH awareness cert from work. I'll go this weekend and produce a written assessment. That also gives us something to show the landowner when we approach them for a stewardship agreement.

On the stewardship question: have we looked at whether the landowner would be open to a community land trust arrangement, or at minimum a licence for temporary use? That would give us standing to improve the site rather than just clear it.

Should we define a minimum evidence standard for signals that get brought to the group? I'm noticing that some signals in the commons are single observations with no corroboration, and it's hard to prioritise against signals that have multiple reports and photographic evidence.

I'd be cautious about setting a minimum threshold — that's exactly how the council has been filtering out complaints for years. A single, well-documented signal from someone who lives next to the problem is worth more than ten form submissions from people who drove past.

What if we distinguish between signals that are sufficient to log a concern and signals that are sufficient to initiate a project? A single signal can raise a concern; a concern needs multiple signals or a pattern before we form a project around it. That seems to match how the platform is intended to work.
That makes sense to me. It also means we can acknowledge every signal without committing to action on each one immediately. The concern stage is where we do the pattern recognition.
